name: Google Maps
description: Design a navigation system like Google Maps with real-time traffic, turn-by-turn directions, and place search.
keyComponents: Tile rendering with vector tiles at multiple zoom levels
Real-time traffic data ingestion and processing
mainChallenges: Handling billions of road segments in the graph
Real-time traffic updates from millions of drivers
Tile pre-computation and CDN caching strategy
Offline maps with limited storage
Multi-modal routing (driving, walking, transit, cycling)
keyPoints: Road network is a weighted directed graph with ~500M segments
A* with heuristics is faster than Dijkstra for point-to-point
Tiles are 256x256px images, pre-rendered at 20+ zoom levels
Traffic data comes from anonymous phone GPS in real-time
Pre-computed routes for common origin-destination pairs
interviewQuestions: How would you store the road network graph?
How does real-time traffic routing work?
How would you design offline maps?
How do you handle multi-modal routing?

name: Google Docs
description: Design a real-time collaborative document editor like Google Docs with multiple cursors and conflict resolution.
keyComponents: Operational Transformation (OT) or CRDTs for conflict resolution
Offline editing with sync on reconnection
mainChallenges: Resolving concurrent edits without conflicts
Maintaining document consistency across clients
Handling offline edits that conflict with online changes
Scaling to thousands of concurrent editors
Cursor position tracking for all collaborators
keyPoints: OT transforms operations to maintain convergence guarantee
CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) avoid central server
Google Docs uses OT with a central server as source of truth
Each character operation gets a unique Lamport timestamp
WebSocket for real-time, with HTTP fallback for firewalls
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between OT and CRDTs?
How would you handle offline editing?
How do you resolve conflicts when two users edit the same line?
How would you scale to 10K concurrent editors?

name: Dropbox / Google Drive
description: Design a file storage and sync system like Dropbox with conflict resolution and delta sync.
keyComponents: Chunked file upload and storage (4MB blocks)
mainChallenges: Efficient sync of large files with many small changes
Resolving conflicts when same file edited offline on multiple devices
Deduplication to save storage across billions of files
Handling version history without exploding storage
Cross-platform sync with varying network conditions
keyPoints: Files split into 4MB chunks, each SHA-256 hashed
Delta sync: only upload changed blocks, not entire file
Deduplication: if block hash exists, just add reference
Conflict: create conflict copy, let user resolve
Metadata tree stored separately from file blocks
interviewQuestions: How would you implement delta sync?
How do you handle file conflicts?
How does deduplication work at scale?
How would you design the sync protocol?

name: Spotify
description: Design a music streaming platform like Spotify with recommendations, playlists, and real-time playback.
keyComponents: Music catalog with metadata and search
Real-time listening activity and social features
mainChallenges: Streaming audio at scale with low latency
Personalized recommendations from billions of listening events
Handling music licensing and regional availability
Offline sync with DRM protection
Social features (listening activity, shared playlists)
keyPoints: Audio files stored in multiple quality tiers (96kbps to 320kbps)
Ogg Vorbis codec for streaming, AAC for podcast
Recommendations: collaborative filtering + NLP on audio features
Pre-fetch next 30 seconds for gapless playback
CDN for audio delivery, edge caching for popular tracks
interviewQuestions: How would you design the recommendation engine?
How do you handle gapless playback?
How would you design offline mode?
How do you handle music licensing at scale?

name: Video Conferencing (Zoom)
description: Design a video conferencing system like Zoom with screen sharing, recording, and breakout rooms.
keyComponents: WebRTC for peer-to-peer and SFU-based video
Selective Forwarding Unit (SFU) for group calls
Screen sharing with dynamic quality adjustment
mainChallenges: Handling 100+ participants in a single call
Maintaining video quality under varying network conditions
Low-latency audio/video synchronization
Screen sharing with minimal lag
Recording and transcription at scale
keyPoints: SFU forwards streams without re-encoding (saves CPU)
Adaptive bitrate: client reports bandwidth, server adjusts quality
Audio prioritized over video (audio packets get QoS)
Simulcast: client sends 3 quality layers, SFU picks best per viewer
SFU clusters for geographic distribution
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between SFU and MCU?
How does adaptive bitrate work for video?
How would you handle 1000 participants in a webinar?
How do you optimize for mobile video calls?
